children.value: The balanced lifestyle is about combining studying and leisure activities. In this part we will show how alcohol consumption and gaming impact education performance.

children.value: The datasets student-math and student_portuguese, both part of the alcohol consumption dataset, show a downward trend in the intensity of drinking, shown on the x-axis, and the percentage of students who participates in drinking that often, shown on the y-axis. A high percentage of the students don’t drink at all (1 on the x-axis) and a very small percentage drinks everyday or almost every day (4.5-5 on the x-axis). This graph gives a clear illustration of the distribution of drinking intensity among students in these datasets. this bar-chart of the datasets also shows that there is no clear correlation between the course, math or Portuguese, and the percentage of students who participate at drinking at a certain intensity.

The datasets student-math and student_portuguese, also show a very subtle trend when it comes to drinking intensity, here also shown on the x-axis, and the final grade at either math or Portuguese, shown on the y-axis. The medians of the final grade related to the alcohol intensity tend to drop slightly when the intensity increases. This is mostly the case at the Portuguese dataset, while we see the median drop from a 6.5 to a 5.0 when the drinking intensity increases from 1 to 5. At the math dataset on the other hand, we see a slight decline of the median from a 5.5 to a 4.0 when the intensity increases from 1 to 3, but after the median increases again to a 5.5 at intensity 5. This can be due to lack of observations for the 4 and 5 intensity in the student math dataset or it can be due to a less powerfull impact on grades when we consider a higher alcohol intensity and maths.
Spending more time gaming will reduce grades, but may improve response times which in turn will improve eduaction performance.

The two graphs suggest that reaction time does not appear to have a strong influence on grades, because the data points do not show a clear or consistent pattern. In contrast, the amount of gaming seems to be more strongly related to grades, since the trend in that graph is more noticeable. Taken together, this means our results do not support the idea that reaction time is an important predictor of grades in this dataset. That is interesting because it seems to contradict

, who reports a different relationship between these factors and academic performance. The paper suggests that reaction time has a positive impact on educational performance because faster reaction times may reflect quicker information processing, better attentional control, or more efficient cognitive functioning, which can support learning and test performance.

Prabu_Kumar_2020.label: Prabu_Kumar_2020
Prabu_Kumar_2020.html: Prabu Kumar, A., Omprakash, A., Kuppusamy, M., K.N., M., B.W.C., S., P.V., V., & Ramaswamy, P. (2020). How does cognitive function measured by the reaction time and critical flicker fusion frequency correlate with the academic performance of students? BMC Medical Education, 20(1). 10.1186/s12909-020-02416-7
